ejercicio.texto: Welcome to the team! Sofía is a young designer from Medellín. She is smart, fast, and very organized. Ben is a tall photographer from London. ejercicio.texto: Ben's British corner, episode one: 'Have got'. In London, my family says it all the time. My mum has got a garden with roses. My dad has got an old car — older than me. My sister has got three cats, and the cats have got strong opinions. Me? I've got a camera, a backpack, and a one-way ticket to adventure. ejercicio.texto: Ben loves giving advice about restaurants. 'Here are my five rules,' he says. 'You should book a table. You should arrive early. You should try the local dish. You should ask the waiter for a recommendation. And you should always leave a tip!' Kenji writes it all down. 'What shouldn't I do?' he asks. Ben smiles. 'You shouldn't order too much — save room for dessert!'

I'd like a quiet weekend at home.' And Ben? 'I would like to try every restaurant in the city! Would you like to come with me?' 'No, thanks,' laughs Sofía. 'I wouldn't like to eat that much!'

ejercicio.texto: There are two ways to talk about the present. Present simple is for your life in general: 'Sofía works in Medellín. Her father is a doctor.' Present continuous is for this exact moment: 'Right now, Sofía is drinking coffee, and her father is examining a patient.' See the difference? 'What do you do?' asks about your job. 'What are you doing?' asks about now. Two questions, two answers!

preguntas.afirmacion: Present continuous is for this exact moment.
'What do you do?' asks about right now.
Present simple is for your life in general.

ejercicio.texto: Kenji writes a blog about his life in Colombia. This week's post: 'The Great Dilemmas of Life'. After three months of serious research, here are my results. Pizza vs burger: pizza is more delicious, but a burger is easier to eat on the bus. YouTube vs Netflix: YouTube is cheaper (it's free!), but Netflix is better for movie nights. Fast food vs homemade food: fast food is faster, obviously, but Abuela Carmen's beans are the most delicious food in Colombia — maybe in the world. Shopping vs sleeping: sleeping is less expensive and more relaxing. Final conclusion: the best things in life are free... like sleeping and YouTube.

'There was a market in the plaza, and there was a lot of noise! We didn't have much money, but there was always some food on the table.' Today there are three supermarkets in her neighborhood, but she still prefers the market. 'The supermarket tomatoes don't have any flavor,' she says. 'And at the market, my friend Rosa always gives me an extra apple.'

When she was a girl, there weren't any supermarkets.

ejercicio.texto: Abuela Carmen has a rule for every situation. Some rules are facts: 'If you cook beans with love, they taste better.' Some rules are warnings: 'If you go out with wet hair, you will catch a cold.' (Science doesn't agree, but nobody argues with Abuela Carmen.) And some rules are dreams: 'If I were president, every town would have a big market and a bigger kitchen.' Ben once asked her: 'What happens if I break one of your rules?' She smiled: 'If you break my rules, you wash the dishes. That one is a fact, my dear.'

Science agrees that wet hair causes colds.
If she were president, every town would have a big market.
If Ben breaks her rules, nothing happens.

ejercicio.texto: Abuela Carmen sold mangoes on the same corner for thirty years, and she says every idiom about money in English has a mango-stand equivalent in her own history. When Sofía complains that a new laptop cost her an arm and a leg, Abuela Carmen nods — she remembers years when a single bad harvest meant paying through the nose for fruit she used to grow herself. His ideas are always good, but they arrive one after another with no connection, like a list instead of an argument. She made him a short list: although and however for contrast, moreover and in addition for adding a point, for example and such as when he needs proof, and therefore or as a result when one idea causes another. ejercicio.texto: Ben has a theory about phrasal verbs with 'get': they follow him around like his phone problems. He gets up late most days, gets by without much money, and somehow always gets away with being late to things that actually matter. When Sofía teases him about losing another phone, he insists he's getting over it — even though this is the fourth time this year. ejercicio.texto: Sofía has started keeping a private list of things that no longer surprise her. Ben losing his phone tops it, followed closely by him arriving fifteen minutes late to everything that isn't actually urgent. Kenji finishing his assignments early, on the other hand, still catches her off guard every time — his studying every single night finally paying off feels less like a surprise and more like something she should have expected from the start. ejercicio.texto: Never had the office seen a week quite like this one. Not only did the client change the deadline without warning, but he also doubled the scope of the project overnight. Rarely does Sofía lose her composure, but by Wednesday even she was struggling. Should anyone have asked Ben for help that week, they would have been surprised — for once, he showed up early every single day. Little did anyone expect the actual solution: Abuela Carmen, tired of hearing the complaints over dinner, simply called the client herself and negotiated a new deadline. Under no circumstances, she told them afterward, should young people forget that a phone call still works better than an anxious group chat.

ejercicio.texto: Having heard the same story about Ben and the rain more times than she can count, Abuela Carmen has started carrying a spare umbrella in her bag, just in case. Walking to the market last week, she ran into him exactly as predicted — soaked, laughing at himself, having once again ignored the forecast. 'Not having checked the weather is basically your whole personality now,' she told him, handing over the umbrella. Having accepted it without argument, Ben promised — not for the first time — that this would be the last time. Having heard that particular promise before too, Abuela Carmen simply smiled and kept walking.

ejercicio.texto: Sofía has learned, slowly, that the biggest mistake in professional writing isn't grammar — it's register. Her first client emails, years ago, swung wildly between 'hey, got your message!' and paragraphs so formal they sounded translated from a legal document. Somewhere in between, she's found, is where real professional writing lives: warm enough to sound human, careful enough to sound competent. She still runs tricky emails past Kenji, who tends toward the overly formal, and Ben, who tends toward the overly casual — between the two of them, she usually lands somewhere sensible. Abuela Carmen's only rule, repeated for years: 'If you wouldn't say it to a stranger's face, don't write it to a stranger either — in either direction.'

ejercicio.texto: Abuela Carmen has strong opinions about how English has softened over the decades. 'When I first learned this language,' she tells Kenji, 'people demanded that things be done, and that was that. Now everyone suggests, and half the time they add should just to sound less bossy.' She isn't wrong: the formal subjunctive — I suggest that he study, it's essential that she be present — has quietly become optional in everyday speech, replaced by should almost everywhere except legal documents and very formal writing. Kenji finds this comforting. 'So if I were you isn't a mistake,' she tells him. 'It's tradition. Use whichever one makes you sound like yourself.'

ejercicio.texto: Given the choice, Sofía would always rather work from home — fewer interruptions, better coffee, no small talk in the hallway. Kenji, on the other hand, would prefer to be surrounded by people; silence makes him restless. Ben rarely has a strong preference either way, though today he'd clearly rather not discuss whatever happened before he arrived. Abuela Carmen, watching all three of them dance around a simple scheduling question, finally said what she'd rather they all understood: it doesn't matter where you work, as long as the work actually gets done. Nobody argued with that.
